en.extract:
The Pangani longclaw (Macronyx aurantiigula) is a species of bird in the family Motacillidae, which includes the pipits and wagtails. It is found in Tanzania, Kenya and Somalia. The bird's natural habitats are dry savanna and subtropical or tropical dry lowland grassland. Primarily insectivorous, it forages in short grasslands and wetlands, using its long claws to scratch for prey such as grasshoppers and beetles. However, habitat loss due to agricultural expansion has led to population declines.
Originally described by German ornithologist Anton Reichenow in 1891, the Pangani longclaw is a member of the longclaw genus Macronyx. The genus name is derived from the Ancient Greek words μακρός/makros "long", and ονυξ/onyx "claw" or "talons". The species name is derived from the Latin words aurantium "orange", and gŭla "throat" or "gullet"


Description
The Pangani longclaw measures around 20 cm (8 in) in length. As its species name suggests, it has a yellow-orange throat. This is bordered with a black necklace. The upperparts are mottled various shades of brown, and the belly is yellow. In immature birds, the belly is more buff than yellow and the dark breast band less distinct. The flanks are streaked black and buff. The flight is jerky. The call has been described as a high-pitched siuuweeeee, with a pitch that rises, falls then rises before fading away.
Its more orange throat helps distinguish it from the yellow-throated longclaw and Abyssinian longclaw, however the throats of female and older immature Pangani longclaws can be more yellow. The last species is also found further north in the Horn of Africa in Ethiopia.
A predominantly ground-dwelling bird, the Pangani longclaw inhabits grasslands, to an altitude of 1800 m (5500 ft).

[[File:Pangani Longclaw P1014553 DxO-1.jpg|thumb|Pangani Longclaw]]
The '''Pangani longclaw''' ('''''Macronyx aurantiigula''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Motacillidae]], which includes the pipits and wagtails. It is found in [[Tanzania]], [[Kenya]] and [[Somalia]]. The bird's natural [[habitat]]s are dry [[savanna]] and subtropical or tropical dry lowland [[grassland]]. Primarily insectivorous, it forages in short grasslands and wetlands, using its long claws to scratch for prey such as grasshoppers and beetles. However, habitat loss due to agricultural expansion has led to population declines.<ref>Mwangi, K., et al. (2018). Threats to endemic birds in Tanzanian savannas. Biodiversity and Conservation, 27(4), 987-1001.</ref>

Originally described by German ornithologist [[Anton Reichenow]] in 1891, the Pangani longclaw is a member of the longclaw genus ''[[Macronyx]]''. The genus name is derived from the [[Ancient Greek]] words μακρός/''makros'' "long", and ονυξ/''onyx'' "claw" or "talons".<ref>{{cite book
  | last1 = Liddell
  | first1 = Henry George
  | author-link = Henry Liddell
  | last2 = Scott
  | first2 = Robert
  | author-link2 = Robert Scott (philologist)
  | pages = [https://archive.org/details/lexicon00lidd/page/423 423, 491]
  | year = 1980
  | orig-date = 1871
  | edition = abridged
  | title = [[A Greek-English Lexicon]]
  | publisher = [[Oxford University Press]]
  | location = Oxford, United Kingdom
  | isbn = 0-19-910207-4
  }}</ref> The species name is derived from the [[Latin]] words ''aurantium'' "orange", and ''gŭla'' "throat" or "gullet"<ref>{{cite book|author = Simpson DP| title = Cassell's Latin Dictionary  | publisher = Cassell Ltd.| year = 1979|edition = 5th|location = London|page=269| isbn=0-304-52257-0}}</ref><!-- aurantium not in there..late latin? -->

==Description==
The Pangani longclaw measures around 20 cm (8 in) in length.<ref name="redman"/> As its species name suggests, it has a yellow-orange throat. This is bordered with a black necklace. The upperparts are mottled various shades of brown, and the belly is yellow. In immature birds, the belly is more buff than yellow and the dark breast band less distinct.<ref name="redman">{{cite book|author1=Nigel Redman |author2=John Fanshawe |author3=Terry Stevenson |title=Birds of the Horn of Africa: Ethiopia, Eritrea, Djibouti, Somalia, Socotra |publisher=A&C Black|year=2009 |page=294|isbn=978-0-7136-6541-3|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ofMlZKqOlisC&q=Pangani+Longclaw&pg=PA294}}</ref> The flanks are streaked black and buff. The flight is jerky.<ref name=eastafrica>{{cite book|author1=Terry Stevenson |author2=John Fanshawe |title=Birds of East Africa|publisher=A&C Black|year=2004|page=302|isbn=0-7136-7347-8|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=pUnSsNTqqmAC&q=Pangani+Longclaw&pg=PA302}}</ref><!-- cites previous four sentences --> The call has been described as a high-pitched ''siuuweeeee'', with a pitch that rises, falls then rises before fading away.<ref name=eastafrica/>

Its more orange throat helps distinguish it from the [[yellow-throated longclaw]] and [[Abyssinian longclaw]], however the throats of female and older immature Pangani longclaws can be more yellow.<ref name="redman"/><ref name=eastafrica/> The last species is also found further north in the Horn of Africa in Ethiopia.<ref name="redman"/>

A predominantly ground-dwelling bird, the Pangani longclaw inhabits grasslands,<ref name="redman"/> to an altitude of 1800 m (5500 ft).<ref name=eastafrica/>
